en.extract:
The Key West quail-dove (Geotrygon chrysia) is a species of bird from the doves and pigeon family Columbidae. It is probably most closely related to the bridled quail-dove.


Distribution and habitat
The Key West quail-dove breeds in the Bahamas and, except for Jamaica, throughout the Greater Antilles.  Despite its name, it does not currently breed in the Florida Keys and southern mainland Florida.
This bird is found in tropical and subtropical dry forests, shrublands, and lowland moist forests.


Diet
These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating seeds, berries and fallen fruit. It is fond of poisonwood fruit.  It will also take snails in its diet.


Breeding
The Key West quail-dove was known to commonly breed in the Florida Keys in the early 1800s, but disappeared from the area in the mid part of that century. However, it occasionally is still recorded in the Keys and southern Florida as a vagrant.  It lays two buff-colored eggs on a flimsy platform built on a shrub.  Some nests are built on the ground.


Description
The Key West quail-dove is approximately 27–31 cm in length.   The bird is distinguished by having a dark rust-colored back and similarly colored wings.  It has some amethyst or bronze green iridescence on its crown, nape and in the back of its neck.  The mantle, back, rump and inner wing coverts show some purplish red iridescence.   It also has a bold white facial stripe.  Its call is a single-pitched moan and is similar that of the white-tipped dove.


References

Field Guide to the Birds of North America. Washington, D.C.: National Geographic Society. 2002. ISBN 978-0-7922-6877-2.
Baptista, Luis; Trail, Pepper W. & Horblit, Helen M. "Family Columbidae (Pigeons and Doves)". In del Hoyo, Josep; Elliott, Andrew & Sargatal, Jordi (eds.). Handbook of the Birds of the World. Vol. 4: Sandgrouse to Cuckoos. Barcelona: Lynx Edicions. ISBN 978-84-87334-22-1.

The '''Key West quail-dove''' ('''''Geotrygon chrysia''''') is a species of [[bird]] from the doves and pigeon family [[Columbidae]]. It is probably most closely related to the [[bridled quail-dove]].<ref name="HBW" >Baptista, L.F., Trail, P.W., Horblit, H.M., Kirwan, G.M. & Boesman, P. (2017). [http://www.hbw.com/species/key-west-quail-dove-geotrygon-chrysia Key West Quail-dove (''Geotrygon chrysia'')]. In: del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A., Sargatal, J., Christie, D.A. & de Juana, E. (eds.). Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona. Accessed: 23 July 2017).</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The Key West quail-dove breeds in [[the Bahamas]] and, except for [[Jamaica]], throughout the [[Greater Antilles]].  Despite its name, it does not currently breed in the [[Florida Keys]] and southern mainland [[Florida]].

This bird is found in [[Tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests|tropical and subtropical dry forests]], [[Tropical and subtropical grasslands, savannas, and shrublands|shrublands]], and [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|lowland moist forests]].

==Diet==
These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating seeds, berries and fallen [[fruit]]. It is fond of [[Metopium toxiferum|poisonwood]] fruit.  It will also take snails in its diet.<ref name="HBW" />

==Breeding==
The Key West quail-dove was known to commonly breed in the Florida Keys in the early 1800s, but disappeared from the area in the mid part of that century.<ref name="northamerica">{{cite book |isbn=9780691117966 |title=Rare Birds of North America |first1=Steve |last1 = Howell |first2=Ian |last2=Lewington |first3=Will |last3=Russel |pages=249-250 |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2014}}</ref> However, it occasionally is still recorded in the Keys and southern Florida as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]].  It lays two buff-colored [[Egg (biology)|eggs]] on a flimsy platform built on a shrub.  Some nests are built on the ground.<ref name="HBW" />

==Description==
The Key West quail-dove is approximately 27–31 cm in length.   The bird is distinguished by having a dark rust-colored back and similarly colored wings.  It has some amethyst or bronze green [[iridescence]] on its crown, nape and in the back of its neck.  The mantle, back, rump and inner wing coverts show some purplish red iridescence.   It also has a bold white facial stripe.  Its call is a single-pitched moan<ref name="northamerica"/> and is similar that of the [[white-tipped dove]].<ref name="HBW" />
